regions: Maine and New England: white pine era (1830s-1880s)
Pacific Northwest: Washington, Oregon, Northern California (1890s-1930s and beyond)
Southern yellow pine belt: Louisiana, Mississippi, East Texas (1880s-1920s)

what_they_did.daily_work: Felling timber with crosscut saws and axes, bucking (cutting felled trees into logs), limbing, and skidding logs to a collection point — a riverbank, a skid road, or a logging railroad spur. River drives were the most dramatic and dangerous phase: loose logs were floated downstream to sawmills, and 'river pigs' (log drivers) rode the logs to keep them moving and break jams. Sawmill workers processed logs into lumber. The industry moved geographically as it exhausted each region's timber — Maine to the Great Lakes to the Pacific Northwest — and the workers moved with it.
what_they_did.seasonal_cycle: In the northern forests, logging was a winter occupation. Trees were felled when the ground was frozen (making it easier to skid logs on ice roads), and logs were stockpiled at riverbanks. Spring thaw triggered the river drive, floating logs to downstream mills. Summer was off-season for most loggers. In the Pacific Northwest and the South, year-round logging was more common due to milder winters.
tools_and_equipment: Peavey and cant hook (for rolling logs)
Springboard (for cutting above the flared base of large trees)
Horse or ox teams for skidding
Logging railroads (replaced river drives in many areas by 1900)
what_they_did.typical_hours: Dawn to dusk — 10-12 hours in winter when daylight was limited. In Pacific Northwest operations with electric lighting, longer shifts were possible. There were no days off in most camps except Sunday, and some operations worked through that.

economics.typical_arrangement: Loggers were paid a monthly wage with room and board provided in company camps. Wages were typically held until the end of the season. Some skilled positions (sawyer, river driver, boom man) earned premium rates. Camp cooks were among the highest-paid workers.
economics.annual_income_range: 1870s-1880s: $26-$40/month plus board. 1890s-1900s: $30-$50/month plus board. 1910s-1920s Pacific Northwest: $3-$5/day. Annual earnings were limited by the seasonal nature of work — a logger who worked a full winter season might earn $150-$300 total in the 1880s-1890s.
economics.comparison: Logging wages were comparable to unskilled factory work on a daily basis, but the seasonal employment, physical danger, and primitive living conditions meant that loggers bore far greater costs for roughly equivalent annual earnings. The appeal was that logging required no capital, no training, and no references — any able-bodied man could walk into a camp and get hired.
economics.debt_cycle: Camp commissaries (the 'wanigan') sold tobacco, clothing, and personal items on credit, deducted from wages at season's end. Some men ended the season owing more than they had earned. In the Pacific Northwest, employment agencies ('man-catchers') in Seattle and Portland charged fees to place loggers in camps, skimming another layer from already thin earnings.

hazards: Logging railroad derailments and equipment failure
Frostbite and hypothermia in northern winter operations
Saw cuts and axe wounds far from medical care
working_conditions.living_conditions: Bunkhouse camps were crude — long, low buildings with double-tier bunks lining the walls, a central stove, and no ventilation. Bedding was straw or hay, rarely washed. Lice, bedbugs, and fleas were universal. Food quality varied enormously by camp — a good cook attracted workers, a bad one drove them away. The camps were isolated, often accessible only by logging railroad or river, with no contact with towns for weeks or months. These men were called 'shanty boys' in the Great Lakes and 'timber beasts' in the Pacific Northwest.
working_conditions.family_involvement: Logging was almost exclusively a single men's occupation. Married loggers left their families for the season. Camp cooks were sometimes women (often the foreman's wife), but the bunkhouse culture was aggressively male. Some lumber company towns (as distinct from logging camps) did include families, particularly around sawmill operations.
working_conditions.autonomy: Within the woods, skilled fallers and sawyers had considerable autonomy — they chose where to make their cuts and managed their own pace. But the camp system was authoritarian: company rules governed behavior, alcohol was officially banned (though widely smuggled), and a man who crossed the foreman was blacklisted across the region.

push_factors: Timber exhaustion — when the forest was cut out, the camp moved or closed
Age and injury — logging was a young man's occupation
The seasonal work cycle left months of unemployment
The IWW (Industrial Workers of the World) organized strikes that sometimes ended in mass firings and blacklisting
Mechanization (chainsaws, tractors, trucks) reduced labor needs after 1930
why_they_left.what_replaced_them: Gasoline-powered chainsaws (1920s-1930s), caterpillar tractors, and logging trucks eliminated the need for large crews, river drives, and logging railroads. A mechanized crew of 5-10 men could harvest timber that had previously required 50-100. The old-growth forests of the Great Lakes were essentially gone by 1920.
why_they_left.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} worked the timber in {county} around {year}, the forest must have seemed inexhaustible. But the industry consumed whole landscapes and then moved on — and the men who cut the trees had to move with it or find something else entirely.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Name, age, birthplace, occupation listed as 'lumberman,' 'logger,' 'sawyer,' 'teamster — logging,' etc.
records_generated.where_to_find: Federal census via Ancestry.com, FamilySearch. State census records where available.
records_generated.research_tip: Loggers were notoriously hard to capture in census records because they were in remote camps during enumeration. Look for them in June (census month) in camp locations, not in towns. Some enumerators recorded entire camps on a single page.

records_generated.record_type: Lumber company payroll and employment records
records_generated.what_it_shows: Employee names, positions, daily or monthly wages, dates of employment, deductions
records_generated.where_to_find: Forest History Society (Durham, NC), Michigan Historical Collections (Bentley Library, U of Michigan), Wisconsin Historical Society, Oregon Historical Society, Minnesota Historical Society
records_generated.research_tip: Surviving company records are the richest source but are unevenly preserved. The Weyerhaeuser Company records at the Minnesota Historical Society are among the most complete. For smaller operations, records were often destroyed when companies dissolved.

records_generated.record_type: State forestry and labor commission reports
records_generated.what_it_shows: Accident reports, inspection findings, labor conditions, camp locations, timber volumes harvested
records_generated.where_to_find: State archives of Michigan, Wisconsin, Minnesota, Washington, Oregon, Maine
records_generated.research_tip: State labor commission reports from the Progressive Era (1900-1920) contain detailed descriptions of logging camp conditions and sometimes name individual workers involved in accidents.

census_clues.what_to_look_for: Look for clusters of young men (18-35) from Scandinavian, French-Canadian, or Irish backgrounds enumerated together in remote townships. A lumberman in one state's census who appears in a different timber region 10 years later likely followed the industry's geographic migration.
census_clues.red_flags: An ancestor listed as 'lumberman' in Michigan in 1880 and 'lumberman' in Washington in 1900 almost certainly followed the industry west as the Great Lakes forests were exhausted. This migration pattern is one of the most common in American logging history.

counter_narratives.claim: Logging was not all Paul Bunyan romance
counter_narratives.context: The folklore of the lumberjack — the mighty Paul Bunyan, the jolly shanty boy singing in the bunkhouse — was largely a later invention, promoted by lumber companies and tourism boards. The reality was brutal, poorly paid, seasonal work performed in primitive conditions. Most loggers did not choose the occupation out of love of the outdoors but because they had no capital, no skills for urban employment, and no alternatives.

counter_narratives.claim: Logging camps were not homogeneously white or American-born
counter_narratives.context: Great Lakes logging camps were profoundly multiethnic — Scandinavians (Norwegians, Swedes, Finns), French-Canadians, Irish, Germans, and Native Americans (particularly Ojibwe) all worked in the woods. In the Pacific Northwest, Japanese and Chinese workers were present in some operations. Southern pine logging employed large numbers of Black workers, often under convict lease arrangements that amounted to forced labor.

counter_narratives.claim: Loggers did not destroy the forests single-handedly
counter_narratives.context: The decisions to clearcut millions of acres of old-growth forest were made by company owners, land speculators, and politicians — not by the men who swung the axes. Loggers were labor, not management. The men who cut the great white pine forests of Michigan and Wisconsin often ended up destitute when the timber was gone and the companies moved on.

source_references.author: Rajala, Richard A.
source_references.title: Clearcutting the Pacific Rain Forest: Production, Science, and Regulation
source_references.notes: Environmental and labor history of Pacific Northwest logging with detailed treatment of working conditions and industry structure

source_references.author: Rohe, Randall
source_references.title: Lumbering: Wisconsin's Northern Urban Frontier
source_references.notes: Published in Wisconsin Magazine of History. Documents the lumber industry's impact on Great Lakes communities and labor

source_references.author: Rector, William Gerald
source_references.title: Log Transportation in the Lake States Lumber Industry, 1840-1918
source_references.notes: Comprehensive study of the logistics of Great Lakes logging including river drives, logging railroads, and the men who operated them

source_references.author: Holbrook, Stewart H.
source_references.title: Holy Old Mackinaw: A Natural History of the American Lumberjack
source_references.notes: Classic popular history written by a former logger. Valuable for its firsthand perspective on camp life, though it leans into the romantic mythology
